title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Into the Storm: A Life Chasing Tornadoes
content.text: When meteorologist Dana Carlisle drives toward a churning, black tornado funnel rather than away from it, she is perfectly calm. Storm chasing, she explains, is not the reckless thrill-seeking that Hollywood films suggest. It is methodical, data-driven fieldwork that has contributed enormously to our understanding of severe weather. (37) [37] Without the efforts of storm chasers, forecasters would have far less information about how tornadoes form and behave. [/37] || Carlisle began chasing storms as a university student in Oklahoma, the heart of America's Tornado Alley. She would load her car with cameras, weather instruments and a laptop streaming radar data, then intercept supercell thunderstorms. (38) [38] Each chase was a carefully planned operation, with hours of preparation for every minute spent near the storm. [/38] The goal was never to get as close as possible, but to position the vehicle in the right place to collect accurate readings. || The data collected by field chasers complements what satellites and fixed weather stations provide. (39) [39] Ground-level measurements of wind speed, pressure and humidity inside a storm are impossible to gather any other way. [/39] This information feeds directly into computer models that generate the tornado warnings which save thousands of lives each year. || Public perception, however, remains dominated by televised 'thrill chasers' racing vehicles toward funnel clouds for dramatic footage. Carlisle is frustrated by this image. (40) [40] Reckless behaviour near tornadoes endangers not only the chasers themselves but also emergency responders who may be called to help them. [/40] || Technology has transformed the profession over the past two decades. High-resolution Doppler radar, drone aircraft and mobile mesonets — vehicles bristling with scientific instruments — have made it possible to collect data that was unimaginable thirty years ago. (41) [41] Yet experienced chasers argue that no instrument yet devised can replace the trained human eye in the field. [/41] || Storm chasing remains an inherently unpredictable pursuit. No two tornadoes behave exactly alike, and the atmosphere can confound even the best models. (42) [42] For Carlisle and her colleagues, that very unpredictability is part of what makes the work so compelling. [/42]

paragraphs.A: This gap between popular myth and scientific reality concerns her deeply.
paragraphs.B: Remote sensing technology has its limits when it comes to capturing local atmospheric conditions.
paragraphs.C: The sheer variety of storm behaviour means that every chase adds something new to the scientific record.
paragraphs.D: Early in her career, she learned that patience and discipline were more important than courage.
paragraphs.E: This is why serious researchers view the sensationalised coverage as actively harmful to the field.
paragraphs.F: Advances in equipment have undoubtedly made the work safer and more productive.
paragraphs.G: It is a discipline that sits at the intersection of science and adventure.

questions.explanation: G describe el storm chasing como una disciplina que combina ciencia y aventura, completando la introducción sobre su valor científico.
D menciona que aprendió que la paciencia y la disciplina son más importantes que el coraje, lo cual conecta con la descripción de la caza como una operación planificada.
B señala las limitaciones de la teledetección, justificando por qué las mediciones a nivel del suelo son imprescindibles.
A indica que la brecha entre el mito popular y la realidad científica le preocupa, conectando con la frustración de Carlisle por la imagen de los 'thrill chasers'.
F reconoce que los avances han mejorado el trabajo, pero el 'yet' que sigue introduce el contraste con la opinión de los chasers experimentados.
C afirma que la variedad de comportamientos de las tormentas enriquece el registro científico, apoyando la idea de que la imprevisibilidad hace el trabajo atractivo.
